questions.text: Two senior team members disagree loudly about the technical approach, and the tension is spreading to the rest of the team. What should the project manager do first?
options: Escalate the disagreement to the project sponsor for a decision
Bring both members together to understand each viewpoint and facilitate a collaborative resolution
Choose the approach favored by the more experienced member to save time
Assign the two members to separate tasks so they no longer interact
questions.explanation: Collaborating/problem-solving addresses the root cause and typically yields the most durable resolution. The PM should facilitate direct dialogue before escalating or forcing a decision.

questions.text: A newly formed team is unsure of roles and looks to the project manager for direction on nearly every task. Which servant-leadership action best fits this stage?
options: Step back and let the team self-organize without guidance
Provide clear direction and remove impediments while the team builds shared norms
Replace team members who ask too many questions
Delay all decisions until the team reaches consensus
questions.explanation: Early (forming) teams need more direction and support. A servant leader provides clarity and removes impediments, gradually shifting to coaching as the team matures.

questions.text: A key stakeholder is consistently unavailable for planned engagement activities. What is the best first step?
options: Remove the stakeholder from the stakeholder register
Proceed without their input to avoid delays
Understand their communication preferences and adjust the engagement approach
Report them to the sponsor as non-cooperative
questions.explanation: Effective stakeholder engagement adapts to each stakeholder's needs and preferences. Understanding why they are unavailable lets the PM tailor the approach rather than exclude them.

questions.text: Midway through execution, the customer requests a change that would expand the deliverable's scope. What should the project manager do first?
options: Implement the change immediately to keep the customer satisfied
Reject the change because the baseline is already approved
Evaluate the change through the integrated change control process
Add the work quietly and absorb it within the current budget
questions.explanation: All changes to a baseline must go through integrated change control so their impact on scope, schedule, cost, and risk is assessed before approval.

questions.text: An activity has an early start of day 4 and a late start of day 9. What is the total float of this activity?
options: 0 days
4 days
5 days
9 days
questions.explanation: Total float = late start − early start = 9 − 4 = 5 days. The activity can slip up to 5 days without delaying the project finish.

questions.text: A project reports EV = 800, PV = 1000, and AC = 900. How is the project performing?
options: Ahead of schedule and under budget
Behind schedule and over budget
On schedule and on budget
Ahead of schedule and over budget
questions.explanation: SV = EV − PV = 800 − 1000 = −200 (behind schedule); CV = EV − AC = 800 − 900 = −100 (over budget). Both variances are negative.

questions.text: New regulations in the industry will affect the project's compliance requirements mid-way through delivery. What should the project manager do?
options: Ignore the regulations until the project is complete
Assess the impact on the project and update plans, risks, and the business case as needed
Cancel the project because requirements changed
Wait for the sponsor to raise the issue before acting
questions.explanation: The PM must monitor the external business environment for compliance and regulatory changes, assess their impact, and adapt the project accordingly to keep it aligned with organizational value.

questions.text: During delivery, the organization's strategic priorities shift and the project's expected benefits are now questionable. What is the best action?
options: Continue as planned because the charter was already approved
Review the business case and benefits with the sponsor to confirm the project is still justified
Immediately terminate the project
Reduce the team size to cut costs
questions.explanation: Projects exist to deliver business value. When strategy shifts, the PM should revisit the business case and benefits realization with the sponsor to confirm continued alignment.

questions.text: A project delivers its scope on time and on budget, but the intended business benefits are not being realized after handover. This is primarily a gap in what?
options: Schedule management
Cost management
Benefits realization and organizational change readiness
Procurement management
questions.explanation: Delivering scope is not the same as realizing value. Sustained benefits depend on benefits realization management and the organization's readiness to adopt the change.
